subject: ap-world-history
theme: Between 1450 and 1750, transoceanic voyages by Portuguese and Spanish explorers connected the Eastern and Western hemispheres for the first time, triggering the Columbian Exchange — a catastrophic transfer of diseases that killed 80–95% of indigenous Americans — while establishing the Atlantic slave trade, plantation agriculture, and mercantilist colonial empires that reorganized global labor, ecology, and commerce into a genuinely integrated world system.
core_concepts: European maritime expansion was enabled by a synthesis of borrowed technologies (the magnetic compass from China, the astrolabe from Islamic astronomy), new ship designs (caravel, carrack, fluyt), accumulated knowledge of wind and current patterns (volta do mar for Atlantic crossings), and state sponsorship of expensive voyages that private merchants could not fund alone.

The Columbian Exchange had profoundly asymmetrical demographic effects: Old World diseases (smallpox, measles, typhus) caused the Great Dying — an 80–95% indigenous population collapse — while New World food crops (potato, maize, sweet potato) eventually enabled long-term population growth across Europe, Africa, and Asia.

European colonial labor systems in the Americas — encomienda (indigenous labor rights), mita (Andean mining draft), and chattel slavery (Africans treated as property) — all represented coercive extraction of labor, differing in form based on local conditions: indigenous population density, production type, and available labor supply after population collapse.

The Atlantic slave trade (1500–1900) transported approximately 12.5 million Africans across the Middle Passage with 12–13% mortality on shipboard alone; total mortality from capture through seasoning was approximately 50%, meaning the system killed roughly as many people as it transported. Some African states (Dahomey, Asante) built power through slave trading, but the trade devastated communities that were raided.

Colonial social hierarchies — casta system in Spanish America, racial codes in English colonies — formalized race-based inequality, with the peninsulares–creoles–mestizos–indigenous–enslaved hierarchy in Spanish colonies reflecting the reality of widespread interracial mixing in contexts of conquest and slavery.

The Manila Galleon trade (1565–1815) created the first genuinely global commodity chain: Andean silver → Acapulco → Manila → China → luxury goods → Spain → Europe, connecting the Americas, Europe, and Asia in a single sustained commercial circuit that historian Dennis Flynn identifies as the structural 'birth of globalization.'

Indigenous and African-descended communities developed strategies of cultural resistance within colonial systems: syncretic religions (Santeria, Vodou, Candomblé) preserved African spiritual traditions within Catholic outward forms; indigenous communities used Spanish colonial courts to protect land rights; enslaved people resisted through rebellion (Nat Turner, Haitian Revolution), work slowdowns, and cultural preservation.
exam_tip: The AP exam frequently asks you to evaluate multiple causes of European colonial expansion — technology, economic incentives, state support, religious motivation — rather than single-cause explanations. Also practice the asymmetry argument for the Columbian Exchange: diseases moved from Old World to New World (catastrophic), food crops moved from New World to Old World (eventually beneficial), and silver moved from New World through Europe to Asia (economically transformative for all three). For DBQ questions on the Atlantic slave trade, remember to address both African agency (states participating as suppliers) and the system's devastating effects, avoiding oversimplified victim or perpetrator narratives.
